insights: Travel expenses have a 41.78% rejection rate—24.98 percentage points higher than the overall 16.8% rate—indicating a severe, category-specific compliance failure that dominates 72.6% of all rejections.
Customer Support accounts for 57.4% of all Travel rejections (35 out of 61) with a 44.3% Travel rejection rate, revealing department-level systemic risk rather than isolated or user-level errors.

Rejected Travel expenses consistently contain equipment/asset-related language (e.g., 'Hardware Asset for Travel Use') and references to 'automatically generated expense line', but these descriptors also appear in processed Travel records—confirming that miscategorization alone does not determine rejection outcomes.

Automatically generated Travel submissions show virtually identical rejection rates to manual ones (43.24% vs 41.28%), ruling out system automation as a root cause and indicating that the rejection logic is applied post-generation, likely during human review or policy validation.

No statistically or operationally meaningful correlation exists between expense amount and rejection status—rejection rates fluctuate across amount bins (42.9%–63.6%) with no threshold effect, confirming that financial thresholds are not driving the pattern.

All major analytical hypotheses—including departmental concentration, description-based categorization, auto-generation, amount thresholds, and data quality—have been systematically ruled out, meaning the root cause of Travel rejections resides outside the current data model or operational metadata captured in the dataset.
summary: The analysis confirms that Travel expenses are the primary driver of rejection risk, with a 41.78% rejection rate—more than double the overall rate—and concentrated heavily in Customer Support (57.4% of Travel rejections). Despite strong textual signals linking rejections to equipment/asset mislabeling and auto-generation language, neither factor reliably distinguishes rejected from processed cases: equipment-related descriptions appear in both groups, and auto-generated submissions reject at nearly the same rate as manual ones. Amount thresholds also show no consistent relationship with rejection, and data quality is confirmed intact across key fields. Critically, all testable explanatory dimensions have been invalidated, indicating the true root cause lies beyond the scope of currently available structured and textual data—most likely in unrecorded reviewer discretion, undocumented policy exceptions, missing approval-stage context, or inconsistent interpretation of 'travel-eligible' expenses during manual adjudication. The next priority is to engage Finance and Compliance stakeholders to audit actual rejection rationale logs, review sample rejection comments, and validate whether policy definitions around 'travel-related equipment' are formally codified and consistently applied.
